title: The Male Fertility Crisis No One Talks About (Plus the Truth About Pregnancy After 35)
guestBio: Vida Delrahim is Co-Founder of WeNatal, a premium prenatal company built for both partners. After 20+ years leading global brand marketing at Nike, she shifted her focus to reproductive health following her own pregnancy losses and has helped 30,000+ couples become parents through science-backed formulation and education.
excerpt: After two devastating miscarriages, former Nike executive Vida Delrahim discovered the shocking truth about male fertility decline and why the pregnancy narrative women receive is completely wrong. This conversation will change how you think about fertility forever.
metaDescription: Former Nike exec Vida Delrahim exposes the male fertility crisis, debunks pregnancy myths after 35, and reveals why trimester zero matters most for conception.
keyTakeaways: Men are 50% of the fertility equation but are rarely tested or held accountable for preconception health.
Sperm counts have declined by 50% in two generations, with projections of zero sperm by 2045 if trends continue.
The term 'geriatric pregnancy' is outdated and harmful - you're not too old at 35 or beyond.
Trimester zero (the 90 days before conception) is the most critical and underutilized window for both partners.
Normal lab results don't mean optimal - you should aim for the top quarter of every range.
MTHFR gene variants affect 40% of the population and can significantly impact fertility outcomes.
The fourth trimester brings severe nutrient depletion that most mothers aren't warned about or supported through.

pullQuotes.text: You are not geriatric. You're doing what's right for you.
Fertility is not a women's health issue. It's a team sport.
Normal is not optimal. Push for the top quarter of every lab range.

timestamps.label: Vida's Story: Two Miscarriages, One Mission
Why Men Are 50% of the Fertility Equation
We Are Animals: Back to Basics

quiz.title: What's Your Fertility Empowerment Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally approach fertility and family planning decisions, and learn which insights from this episode will serve you best.

A.name: The Researcher
A.description: You believe knowledge is power and want to understand every detail before making decisions. You're the person who reads studies, compares lab ranges, and asks your doctor detailed questions. Your thorough approach is a strength that can lead to optimal outcomes. This episode will give you the scientific backing you crave and help you understand why pushing for optimal lab ranges and including your partner in preconception health is so crucial.

B.name: The Collaborator
B.description: You instinctively know that fertility is a team effort and naturally include your partner in health and family planning decisions. You understand that this journey affects both of you and should involve both of you. Your inclusive mindset is exactly what the fertility world needs more of. This episode will validate your approach and give you specific ways to optimize both partners' health during trimester zero.

C.name: The Advocate
C.description: You're tired of outdated medical advice and ready to challenge the narratives that don't serve women. You question terms like 'geriatric pregnancy' and push back against fear-based messaging about fertility. Your willingness to advocate for yourself and others is powerful and necessary. This episode will arm you with the research and language you need to continue fighting for better fertility care and more empowering conversations.

D.name: The Optimizer
D.description: You focus on what you can control and believe in preparing your body and mind for the best possible outcomes. You're interested in nutrition, lifestyle factors, and creating ideal conditions for success. Your proactive approach to health optimization is exactly what trimester zero is all about. This episode will give you specific strategies for maximizing both partners' fertility potential and understanding the true timeline for reproductive health.

questions.question: When you hear conflicting advice about fertility and pregnancy, your first instinct is to:

options.text: Research the latest studies and compare different medical perspectives
Discuss it with your partner and get their thoughts on what feels right
Question whether the advice is based on outdated information or fear
Focus on what you can actually control and take action on

questions.question: If you received lab results that were 'normal' but not optimal, you would:

options.text: Ask for the specific numbers and research what optimal ranges should be
Discuss with your partner how this might affect both of your health goals
Challenge your doctor about why they're not aiming for optimal health
Create a plan to improve those numbers through diet and lifestyle changes

questions.question: When planning for a potential pregnancy, you believe it's most important to:

options.text: Understand all the science behind preconception health and fertility
Make sure both partners are equally invested and prepared
Reject outdated timelines and pressure from society or family
Optimize health and nutrition for at least three months beforehand

questions.question: Your biggest frustration with current fertility advice is:

options.text: It lacks detailed scientific explanations and specific protocols
It focuses too much on women and ignores the male partner's role
It uses fear-based language that makes women feel broken or limited
It doesn't provide actionable steps for improving outcomes

questions.question: If you learned about a potential fertility issue, your approach would be to:

options.text: Deep dive into research about causes, treatments, and success rates
Bring your partner into the conversation and work through it together
Seek out providers who don't see you as a problem to be solved
Immediately start implementing lifestyle changes to improve the situation

questions.question: Your ideal approach to family planning involves:

options.text: Having all the data and understanding exactly what to expect
Making decisions as a team with equal input from both partners
Ignoring societal timelines and doing what's right for your life
Preparing your mind and body to be in the best possible condition

transcript: Let's have some fun. >> Today's guest is someone who is quietly leading a revolution and how we think about fertility, health, and preparation for motherhood and honestly for life. I'm sitting down with Vita Del Raheem, co-founder of We Natal, a company that was born not out of theory, but out of lived experience, deep questioning, and a refusal to accept normal as good enough. We Natal wasn't built from theory. It was built after both founders experienced miscarriage and refused to accept no answers as an outcome. That moment became the catalyst for a deeper investigation into what we're being told about fertility, about the role of both partners, and about the gap between what's considered normal and what's actually optimal. In this conversation, we're going to talk about why normal lab results don't always mean optimal health, the global fertility crisis that no one's talking about, why men are 50% of the fertility equation, and why they've been left out, and something called trimester zero, the 3 months before conception, and that may matter more than anything else. This conversation is part science, part story, and part wakeup call. And if you've ever thought about having children or supporting someone who is, this episode is essential. Vita, thank you so much for being here on the manager today. So, let's start. Could you tell me your story? It's such a powerful entry into this conversation. Can you take us back to what led you to start Weedle? >> Yes. And thank you for having me. I'm so excited for our chat and totally feel connected to what a powerhouse you are. So, it's funny because like many founders, it wasn't really in our road map. Ronit and I became best friends at our Nike corporate careers over 20 years ago. Literally going through our young single lives together, climbing our corporate ladders, dating at the same time, getting married around the same time and always thought we might once want to work together because we were so connected, but never really knew what would come out of the heartbreak of our pregnancy losses. And so how it began was around 8 years ago, Ronit and I were at Nike. I I specifically was leading massive global events for Nike. And my job was really to think through the athletes, you know, every footstep and how to make them from good to great and knowing what prep goes into making athletes amazing and what sometimes shaving an ounce of off their shoe or apparel could be the difference between gold and silver. And so I spent my life being, you know, so conscious of the details and what it takes to be great at your craft. And when it came to pregnancy, I treated it like a casual jog. I was like, we're young, we're healthy, you know, we've been married a couple years, we should just start trying, like many of us, and got pregnant, went in for a 14-week ultrasound, which should have been a routine checkup. I had already told everyone because I was past the first trimester which many of us are told you're safe after the first trimester only to see my OB/GYN's face drop. the room goes silent and he looked at me and just said, "I'm so sorry. There's no heartbeat." >> And words, I will never forget the worst. The worst, the heartbreak, >> the, you know, driving home crying so hard, your jaw aches. And for me specifically, I started scrutinizing everything I've ever done from the stress at work to maybe the workout, the Soul Cycle class. Was it too much caffeine because I love coffee? Was it? You know, I could have picked any of those things, but I I went into this deep depression wondering what I did to cause it or deserve it. >> Mhm. >> Luckily for me, Ronit was my best friend. She had just had her daughter. She was now working for Dr. Mark Hyman, who is a big name in functional medicine, and she encouraged me to go beyond everything. You know, my doctor just looked at me and said, you know, it's very common. It's normal. Just keep trying when you're ready. But she said, you know, look beyond your labs. Look beyond what they're saying is normal. and she encouraged me and lo and behold found out I have Hashimoto's which is a thyroid disorder worked on my you know diet lifestyle nutrition got everything in order and in that process of going deeper I was able to get myself in a healthier state and have my baby girl Dalia who's now 8 years old and you know I thought the nightmare was over I thought I had learned everything about prenatal care I am now taking care of myself I'd removed more toxins from my world to fast forward to spring of 2020 amidst the COVID chaos. We were we were both trying for our second. Went in for another routine checkup to get that same deafening silence. And I was there having my second miscarriage and just dismissed just told you know it just happens. The only thing that changed for me was a week later my best friend calls me and the same exact thing happened to her >> and I knew she had the perfect prenatal care routine. She was working for the best doctors. She knew everything to do and she was given the same, you know, probably her age. She was 41 at the time, you know, she was given no answers. But thank goodness she was working in the functional medicine space, so she knew there's more to the story. And she started doing crazy amounts of research and uncovered, you know, the the stats about men being 50% of pregnancy health. And men literally on highdose antioxidants four to five times being able to improve the likelihood of their partner having not only a healthy pregnancy, a healthy child and giving them, you know, their lifelong genetics. And I remember Amy, the day she called me, I remember what I where I was and what I was wearing because it was so basic. It's like intuitive. We know it takes two to make a baby. But the fact that I had had multiple losses, my husband was standing in the room for both of them. And not a single person turned to him to even ask him a question about his diet, his lifestyle. And here he was living his best life, doing whatever he wanted. I was literally analyzing the shampoo and the skin care I put on my body just like something. >> It's the same thing. >> Literally, that stopped us dead in our tracks. We said, "Why is no one talking about men's health? Why is there not men's prenatalss on the market, especially with the research about these highdosese antioxidant?" >> So, what we did, Ron put us on a protocol. She put our husbands on a protocol with all the research. The men agreed to, you know, a couple months, 3 months, and we'll talk about trimester zero of no toxic behaviors, no, you know, cleaning it up. We need to educate about the optimism of having kids later in life and the joys of it and not I was called geriatric. And so that became our why. So anyone could put out anything and it's scary. >> Yes. As long as it's generally regarded as safe grass, you are they're basically unregulated. When we brought Malicious to market, I was shocked um at the process in the US versus the process in Canada. Because in Canada, you have to go through Health Canada. They look at every single ingredient and who you're buying it from, where it's sourced from, every single ingredient that's in your product. We don't have that with the FDA here. We have some of the best doctors who formulated based on clinical research. It's a just so such an important way that we celebrate women in seeing that, you know, our life experiences shape the what we do and how we influence the world and how we're able to help other people with the companies that we form. So, just in general, when we get our lab work done, our physical once a year, we're getting labs and the range is massive. I then went to specialists. Like, there's so much we do to prep for a wedding day or even our nursery or our baby shower. And so that's actually counter to what you think if even if you are having kids later in life, which by the way is the new normal. More women are having kids in their 40s than their 20s. So I want everyone to hear that that it's not you're not geriatric. You are doing what's right for you. >> Yeah. I had three kids over 35. I had one at 35. I had one at 40. content:
What if everything you've been told about fertility is wrong? What if the narrative that women's fertility "drops off a cliff" at 35 is not only outdated but actively harmful? And what if there's a male fertility crisis happening right now that could change the future of human reproduction forever?

These are the questions that former Nike executive Vida Delrahim found herself asking after experiencing two devastating miscarriages with no medical answers. Her journey from corporate marketing leader to fertility advocate isn't just inspiring—it's revolutionary. And it's exactly the kind of conversation that changes how you see everything.

The Fertility Narrative That's Failing Women

Let's start with the most damaging myth in reproductive health: that fertility is primarily a women's issue. Vida's research reveals something shocking—men contribute to 50% of fertility challenges, yet they're rarely tested, rarely held accountable for preconception health, and rarely included in the fertility conversation until there's already a problem.

Even more alarming is what Dr. Shanna Swan calls "spermageddon"—the dramatic decline in male fertility that's happening at an unprecedented rate. Sperm counts have dropped by 50% in just two generations. If current trends continue, projections suggest sperm counts could effectively reach zero by 2045. Read that again. We're potentially looking at a fertility crisis that could fundamentally change human reproduction, and hardly anyone is talking about it.

This isn't just about numbers on a lab report. This is about the toxic load we're all carrying—the plastics, petroleum products, and processed foods that are disrupting our hormonal systems and reproductive health in ways we're only beginning to understand.

Reframing Pregnancy After 35

If you've ever been told you're having a "geriatric pregnancy," Vida wants you to know something: you are not geriatric. You're making decisions that are right for your life, your career, and your family planning timeline. The term itself is not only outdated but actively harmful to women who are perfectly capable of having healthy pregnancies well into their thirties and beyond.

The real issue isn't your age—it's that we've been operating with incomplete information. We've been focusing on the wrong partner, the wrong timeline, and the wrong metrics. Instead of obsessing over a woman's biological clock, we should be looking at both partners' health optimization during what Vida calls "trimester zero."

The Power of Trimester Zero

Trimester zero—the 90 days before conception—may be the most critical and underutilized window in reproductive health. This is when both partners can optimize their health, address nutritional deficiencies, reduce toxic load, and create the best possible environment for conception and healthy pregnancy.

Here's what most people don't realize: men regenerate sperm every 72 days, which means they have the power to dramatically improve sperm quality in just a few months. Meanwhile, women can address issues like MTHFR gene variants (which affect 40% of the population), optimize nutrient levels, and ensure their bodies are truly ready for the demands of pregnancy.

But here's the catch—most healthcare providers aren't looking at optimal ranges, they're looking at normal ranges. Vida's experience taught her to push for the top quarter of every lab range, not just values that fall within the broad spectrum of "normal." Normal isn't optimal, and when it comes to fertility and pregnancy, optimal matters.

The Fourth Trimester Crisis

The conversation doesn't end at birth. Vida sheds light on something most new mothers experience but rarely discuss—the severe nutrient depletion that happens during the fourth trimester. Your body has just spent nine months building a human being, then potentially breastfeeding, and yet there's virtually no medical protocol for replenishing what you've given.

This depletion isn't just about feeling tired (though that's certainly part of it). It's about understanding that your body has literally transferred its nutrient stores to your baby, and without intentional replenishment, you're starting your recovery—and potentially your next pregnancy—at a deficit.

Building a Business on Truth, Not Trends

What makes Vida's story even more compelling is how she and her co-founder built WeNatal. Instead of taking venture capital that might compromise their formulations, they bootstrapped the company to maintain complete control over product integrity. They chose education over flashy marketing, science over trends, and long-term impact over quick profits.

This approach reflects something deeper about how we need to think about fertility and reproductive health. It's not about quick fixes or trendy solutions. It's about understanding the science, optimizing both partners' health, and taking a comprehensive approach that honors the complexity of human reproduction.

What This Means for You

Whether you're thinking about starting a family, expanding your family, or supporting someone who is, this conversation changes everything. It means shifting from a mindset of scarcity and fear to one of empowerment and optimization. It means including your partner in the conversation from day one. It means pushing your healthcare providers for optimal, not just normal, and it means understanding that fertility isn't just about timing—it's about creating the best possible conditions for success.

Most importantly, it means recognizing that you have more control than you've been told. The narrative of declining fertility and limited options doesn't have to be your story. With the right information, the right support, and the right approach, you can rewrite that narrative entirely.
